[Notre Dame Research](/ "Homepage shortcut key = 1")

# Notre Dame researchers develop new, ultra-power-efficient 5G antenna

Published: March 12, 2025

Author: [Brett Beasley](/news-and-events/news/authors/brett-beasley/)

![A hand holds a small, circular, white object with a textured, woven pattern. The backdrop is a dark blue surface with a repeating, geometric pattern of concave shapes.](/assets/608656/1400x/feature.jpg)

Globally, two billion people use fifth-generation (5G) wireless networks. These users have gained more rapid upload and download speeds, lower latency, and greater reliability on their mobile devices. But the rollout of 5G technology has also come with a steep energy cost. 5G networks require more energy than previous generations, [with each base station consuming as much energy as 73 U.S. households](https://envirotecmagazine.com/2021/11/08/how-green-is-5g/).

![A person holds a small, white, circular object. The object appears to be made of a porous, textured material, resembling a 3D-printed structure or filter. The focus is on the object, while the person and background are slightly blurred.](/assets/608655/photo2.jpg)

Now, with funding from the U.S. Army, researchers at the University of Notre Dame are launching an effort that could help reverse this trend. The team will apply [prior research](https://microwavelab.nd.edu/publications/) conducted at the University on the physics of low-power antennas. Working with a set of industry partners, they aim to produce an antenna that delivers 5G-level performance while using less than ten percent of the energy.

The team is led by [Jonathan Chisum](https://wireless.nd.edu/people/faculty/jonathan-chisum/), an associate professor in the Department of Electrical Engineering and an affiliate of Notre Dame's [Wireless Institute](https://wireless.nd.edu/). Chisum says the key to the new antenna is a kind of artificial dielectric material designed and built in his lab.

“Right now, a large portion of the cost to operate a cellular network is for electricity. If you look at a cell tower, you can see why: It uses a different antenna for each band, and these rely on active, powered chips,” Chisum said. “Our initial idea was simple: What if we could design similar capabilities into just one very wideband antenna by letting the physics of materials do the work normally done by many power-hungry chips.”

![A person holds a large, round, light gray disc. The disc appears to be made of a layered material, with a visible textured pattern along its outer edge. A small circular hole is present in the center of the disc's top surface.](/assets/608666/1400x/image.jpg)

The new low-power antenna is a type of millimeter-wave gradient index (GRIN) lens antenna. Although GRIN lenses have existed for over a century, the idea of developing a GRIN lens antenna for 5G networks once seemed far-fetched to most researchers in the field of wireless technology. However, over the past eight years, Chisum and his lab have made groundbreaking discoveries in the fundamental science of wideband beam steering. These findings have allowed Chisum and his team to create one antenna that can operate over all the frequency bands for 5G, a feat once thought to be impossible.

The antenna’s wideband, low-power capabilities make it especially useful to the U.S. Army. The Army was involved in the development of 5G technology and relies on it not just for secure communications but also for tracking equipment and monitoring the health of soldiers. However, current 5G technologies are difficult and costly to set up, transport, and operate in the field.

“The Army has to operate 5G networks all over the world,” Chisum explained, “and 5G networks operate at different frequencies across the globe. Thus, a wideband solution like ours is an essential capability. And since it consumes very little power and is relatively small and lightweight, it can be integrated into a mobile platform.”

Once it is implemented, the technology will provide a “5G-on-the-move” solution with improved efficiency, safety, and versatility.

Chisum also emphasized that developing this new technology is the first step in integrating it into mobile networks for civilian use.

“The deployment of 5G millimeter-wave base stations in current 5G networks has stalled because operators cannot afford the cost of the current multi-antenna solutions. However, wideband 5G antennas based on GRIN lenses open up new possibilities for lowering cost and efficiency in commercial wireless networks,” Chisum said.

So far, Chisum and his team have a working prototype of their design produced in the lab one thin layer at a time through a detailed 100-hour process. The team is developing an efficient and cost-effective way to manufacture the device using cutting-edge 3D printing technology. It will also allow Chisum’s team to demonstrate the technology in the field, paving the way for inclusion in a 5G network.

![A 3D model of a gray cube with a gyroid infill pattern on a dark gray background. The intricate design creates a visually appealing and complex structure.](/assets/608662/1400x/try.png)

To transition this technology from the lab into the field, Chisum’s lab has formed a team of industry partners. The team will include multiple leading vendors with expertise in wireless networks, antennas, and additive manufacturing uniquely suited to GRIN media.

"Nicolas Garcia, CEO of Cheshir Industries, said, “The Cheshir Industries team is proud and excited to lead the lens and array design efforts for Notre Dame's 5G-on-the-move antenna development program. This project represents not only an important step in advancing our nation’s wireless capabilities but also a major milestone in the commercialization and development of wideband GRIN antenna systems.” Cheshir Industries was launched with support from Notre Dame’s IDEA Center. It was co-founded by Chisum along with two graduates of Notre Dame’s Electrical Engineering doctoral program: Nicolas Garcia (‘22 Ph.D.) and Nicholas Estes (‘22 Ph.D.)."

Karlo Delos Reyes, Chief Customer Officer and Co-founder at Fortify, said, “As the leader in RF design and manufacturing, we at 3D Fortify are thrilled to collaborate with the University of Notre Dame and our industry partners to deliver cutting-edge technology. This partnership allows us to leverage our advanced capabilities to push the boundaries of what is possible in GRIN lens antenna design. Together, we’re working across the value chain to deliver a transformative solution that will pave the way for future civilian applications."

To learn more about advances in wireless technology at the University of Notre Dame, visit <wireless.nd.edu>.

**Contact:**

**About Notre Dame Research:**

The University of Notre Dame is a private research and teaching university inspired by its Catholic mission. Located in South Bend, Indiana, its researchers are advancing human understanding through research, scholarship, education, and creative endeavor in order to be a repository for knowledge and a powerful means for doing good in the world. For more information, please see <research.nd.edu> or @[UNDResearch](https://www.linkedin.com/company/undresearch/).

# Academic Research and the 5G/6G Innovation Testbed: An Insight

The world of academic research is continuously evolving, driven by the relentless pursuit of knowledge and innovation. In this ever-changing landscape, the 5G/6G Innovation Testbed is an exemplar of cutting-edge technology that enables researchers to push the boundaries of what is possible. This state-of-the-art facility serves as a collaborative hub, where brilliant minds from various disciplines can explore the vast potential of next-generation wireless communication technologies.

Within the confines of the 5G/6G Innovation Testbed, researchers are granted unprecedented access to a comprehensive suite of tools and resources. From complex simulations to real-world testbeds, they can analyze and validate concepts and ideas, paving the way for groundbreaking discoveries. The testbed’s versatility allows for the smooth integration of diverse research domains, fostering interdisciplinary collaboration and ideas from different entities.

The 5G/6G Innovation Testbed acts as a bridge between academia and industry, fostering a symbiotic relationship that accelerates the translation of research findings into tangible, real-world applications. By providing a platform for industry partners to engage with cutting-edge research, the testbed facilitates the commercialization of innovative technologies, driving economic growth and societal advancement.

### Introduction to Academic Research in 5G/6G

The world of [wireless communication](https://spectrum.ieee.org/ai-for-wireless) has witnessed a remarkable transformation, propelled in no small part by academic research. As society’s desire for efficient connectivity and high-speed data transfer continues to grow, the innovations originating within academic institutions have become a driving force in the evolution of 5G and 6G technologies.

The significance of academic research in 5G and 6G innovation cannot be overstated. Universities and research centers serve as incubators for groundbreaking ideas, fostering an environment of intellectual curiosity and scientific rigor. It is within these institutions of knowledge that the theoretical foundations for next-generation wireless communication technologies are laid, paving the way for practical applications that may one day revolutionize the way people live, work, and communicate.

Academic institutions have been at the forefront of the development of 5G technology, contributing many invaluable insights and breakthroughs. From the exploration of millimeter-wave frequencies to the advancement of massive MIMO (Multiple-Input Multiple-Output) techniques, researchers have tirelessly pushed the boundaries of what was once thought possible. Their efforts have culminated in the creation of a robust 5G ecosystem, enabling lightning-fast data speeds, ultra-low latency, and unprecedented network capacity.

Moreover, the collaborative nature of academic research has facilitated the mutual exchange and integration of ideas, fostering interdisciplinary approaches that have yielded innovative solutions to complex challenges. Industry-academia partnerships have further amplified the impact of these endeavors, ensuring an effortless translation of theoretical concepts into practical, market-ready technologies.

As the world eagerly anticipates the advent of 6G, the academic community is already exploring uncharted territories, driven by the promise of 6G’s capabilities and potentially transformative applications. What sets 6G apart from its predecessor, 5G, in academic exploration is its vision of attaining universal connectivity, seamlessly merging the physical and digital spheres.

Researchers are exploring cutting-edge concepts such as Terahertz (THz) communications, intelligent reflecting surfaces, and the integration of [5G advanced](https://www.us-ignite.org/program/platforms-for-advanced-wireless-research/) features with emerging technologies like artificial intelligence, blockchain, and quantum computing. These explorations aim to unlock new frontiers in wireless communication, enabling revolutionary use cases that transcend our current limitations.

The pursuit of 6G is not merely an incremental advancement but a paradigm shift that challenges conventional wisdom and pushes the boundaries of what is possible. Academic institutions are at the vanguard of this transformative journey, fostering multidisciplinary collaborations and nurturing the next generation of visionary thinkers and innovators.

### Key Research Areas in 5G/6G Innovation

The evolution of wireless communication technologies has delivered unprecedented connectivity, transforming the fundamental structure of the digital environment. As the world embraces the potential of 5G and eagerly anticipates the advent of 6G, academic institutions find themselves at the forefront of this technological revolution, spearheading research endeavors that will shape the future of [wireless internet](https://www.nist.gov/programs-projects/advanced-security-architectures-next-generation-wireless) and redefine the boundaries of what is possible.

Multiple domains are currently focal points for academic investigation within 5G and 6G technologies. One of the most prominent areas of exploration is the optimization of network architectures, encompassing the seamless integration of heterogeneous networks, intelligent resource allocation, and the development of self-organizing and self-healing systems.

Academia is also actively engaged in pushing the boundaries of spectrum efficiency, investigating novel techniques such as advanced coding, modulation schemes, and interference management strategies. The quest for higher data rates and lower latency has led researchers to explore millimeter-wave and Terahertz communications, unlocking new frontiers in bandwidth utilization.

The insatiable demand for higher data rates and increased network capacity has prompted academia to explore new spectrum options for [6G technology](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9061504). Researchers are venturing into uncharted territories, investigating the feasibility of utilizing higher frequency bands, such as Terahertz and visible light communication (VLC), to supplement the existing spectrum resources.

Academic institutions are leading the charge in developing innovative solutions to address the challenges associated with these new spectrum domains, such as atmospheric absorption, beam steering, and signal processing techniques. By unlocking the potential of previously untapped frequency ranges, researchers aim to pave the way for unprecedented data throughput and enable a multitude of new applications and services.

Furthermore, academia plays a pivotal role in conducting comprehensive spectrum studies, analyzing the impact of spectrum allocation on various industries and use cases. These efforts inform policymakers and regulatory bodies, ensuring that the allocation of new spectrum bands for 6G is guided by scientific evidence and aligns with the overarching goals of promoting innovation and maximizing societal benefits.

However, as the world becomes increasingly interconnected, security concerns associated with 5G and the upcoming 6G networks have taken center stage. Academic researchers are actively addressing these challenges, pioneering robust security protocols, encryption techniques, and advanced authentication mechanisms to safeguard the integrity and privacy of data transmission.

A key area of focus in network security is the development of secure and resilient network architectures, capable of withstanding cyber threats and ensuring uninterrupted service delivery. Researchers are exploring cutting-edge technologies such as blockchain, quantum cryptography, and advanced access control mechanisms to fortify the security posture of 5G and 6G networks.

Academia plays a crucial role in fostering a culture of security awareness and education, equipping the next generation of professionals with the knowledge and skills necessary to navigate the ever-evolving cybersecurity landscape.

Beyond spectrum and security concerns, academic institutions are also exploring the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) into 5G and 6G systems. Researchers are pursuing the development of intelligent algorithms that can adapt to dynamic network conditions, optimize resource allocation, and enhance user experiences through predictive analytics and personalized services.

The academic community’s contributions extend beyond the technical aspects of 5G and 6G technologies. Researchers are actively engaged in exploring the socio-economic implications of these advancements, studying their potential impact on various sectors, such as healthcare, transportation, and manufacturing. This holistic approach ensures that the development and deployment of 5G and 6G are guided by a deep understanding of their broader ramifications, fostering responsible innovation and sustainable growth.

### Collaborations Between Academia and Industry

In the domains of 5G and 6G innovation, the synergy between academic institutions and industry partners has proven to be a driving force behind technological advancement. These collaborations represent a symbiotic relationship, where the theoretical rigor of academia intersects with the practical expertise of industry, fostering an environment conducive for groundbreaking discoveries and real-world applications.

Academic institutions collaborate with industry partners in various ways to drive 5G/6G innovation. One key approach is the establishment of [collaborative research](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9254613) centers or joint laboratories, where researchers from both academia and industry work side by side, pooling their collective knowledge and resources. These shared facilities provide a conducive environment for collaborative research, enabling interdisciplinary teams to tackle complex challenges and explore novel solutions.

Moreover, academic institutions often engage in sponsored research projects, where industry partners provide funding and real-world problem statements, allowing researchers to apply their expertise to address specific industry needs. This mutually beneficial arrangement ensures that academic research remains relevant and aligned with the practical demands of the market.

Another vital aspect of academia-industry collaboration is the exchange of knowledge and talent. Academic institutions frequently host industry representatives as guest lecturers or adjunct faculty members, fostering a cross-pollination of ideas and exposing students to real-world perspectives. Conversely, industry partners offer internships, co-op programs, and employment opportunities to talented graduates, ensuring a seamless transition of innovative ideas from the academic realm to the commercial sphere.

The intersection of academic and industrial research in the field of 5G/6G innovation presents both challenges and benefits. One of the primary challenges lies in the inherent differences between the two cultures. Academia prioritizes theoretical exploration and the pursuit of knowledge, while industry often focuses on practical applications and commercial viability. Reconciling these divergent priorities and finding common ground can be a delicate balance.

However, these challenges are outweighed by the numerous benefits that arise from such collaborations. The interplay of ideas and perspectives fosters a conducive environment for innovation, where academic insights are enriched by industry’s practical experience, and industry’s challenges are illuminated by academic rigor. This convergence of expertise accelerates the pace of [technological advancement](https://www.theengineer.co.uk/content/opinion/guest-blog-accelerating-technological-advances-with-industry-academia-collaboration/) and facilitates the development of cutting-edge solutions that address real-world needs.

Furthermore, collaborative research efforts between academia and industry often result in shared intellectual property, fostering a mutually beneficial ecosystem where both parties can capitalize on their combined labor.

Collaborations between academia and industry can expedite the practical implementation of academic findings in several ways. Firstly, industry partners provide valuable feedback and real-world validation of academic research, ensuring that theoretical concepts are grounded in practical considerations and market realities. This iterative process refines and shapes academic findings, increasing their relevance and applicability.

Furthermore, industry partners possess the necessary infrastructure, manufacturing capabilities, and distribution channels to commercialize and scale academic innovations. By leveraging these resources, the journey from theoretical concept to market-ready product or service is significantly accelerated, bridging the gap between research and implementation.

Additionally, industry collaborations often facilitate access to funding and investment opportunities, enabling the further development and refinement of academic findings. This financial support empowers researchers to continue their explorations, overcome technical hurdles, and transform their ideas into tangible, market-ready solutions.

The collaborative efforts between academia and industry can create a virtuous cycle of innovation, where theoretical insights inform practical applications, and real-world challenges inspire new avenues of academic inquiry. The continuous exchange of knowledge and expertise can propel the rapid advancement of 5G and 6G technologies, ensuring that the benefits of these groundbreaking innovations are realized and implemented in a timely and impactful manner.

### Testbed Infrastructure for 5G/6G Research

In the rapidly evolving landscape of [wireless connectivity](https://advancedwireless.org/pawr-program-announces-successful-demonstration-of-intelligent-spectrum-sharing-technology-developed-by-zylinium-research-in-collaboration-with-the-u-s-department-of-defense/), the ability to validate and refine emerging technologies is paramount. The IEEE 5G/6G Innovation Testbed is a pioneering initiative, designed to propel the development and commercialization of next-generation wireless communication systems. This state-of-the-art facility serves as a catalyst for rapid innovation, enabling researchers, industry partners, and academic institutions to collaborate and explore the vast potential of 5G and 6G technologies.

The primary purpose of the IEEE 5G/6G Innovation Testbed is to provide a comprehensive and flexible platform for the testing, validation, and optimization of cutting-edge wireless communication technologies. By offering a controlled and configurable environment, the Testbed empowers researchers and developers to conduct rigorous evaluations, identify performance bottlenecks, and refine their solutions before deploying them in real-world scenarios.

The Testbed enables rapid innovation by offering a unique combination of advanced hardware, software, and virtualized infrastructure. Its modular design allows for seamless integration of various components, enabling researchers to construct tailored testing environments that accurately mimic real-world conditions. This flexibility facilitates iterative development cycles, where ideas can be quickly prototyped, tested, and refined, accelerating the pace of innovation and time-to-market for new products and services.

Moreover, the Testbed’s open architecture encourages collaboration and knowledge sharing among diverse stakeholders, fostering an environment where ideas can interplay and synergies can be leveraged. By breaking down traditional barriers, the Testbed promotes interdisciplinary research and fosters the development of innovative solutions that transcend individual domains.

For 5G testing, the Testbed offers a comprehensive suite of capabilities that cater to the diverse requirements of researchers and developers. One of the key features is the support for [Open RAN](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10024837) (Open Radio Access Network) architectures, enabling the evaluation of disaggregated and virtualized RAN solutions. This capability is crucial for exploring new paradigms in network architecture and paving the way for more flexible, cost-effective, and scalable 5G deployments.

Additionally, the Testbed provides advanced tools for performance testing, network slicing, and end-to-end orchestration, allowing researchers to assess the real-world impact of their solutions on various network conditions and use cases. This comprehensive testing environment ensures that 5G technologies are thoroughly validated before commercial deployment, mitigating potential risks and ensuring a smooth user experience.

The versatility of the IEEE 5G/6G Innovation Testbed enables a wide range of testing and development activities. Researchers can conduct rigorous performance evaluations, stress-testing the limits of their solutions under various load conditions and network configurations. Furthermore, the Testbed facilitates the development and testing of new applications and services, enabling researchers to explore innovative use cases and validate their viability in a controlled environment.

Interoperability testing is another critical aspect of the Testbed’s capabilities, ensuring that emerging technologies can seamlessly integrate with existing infrastructure and operate harmoniously within the larger ecosystem. This capability is particularly valuable for industry partners, enabling them to validate the compatibility of their solutions with third-party components and standards.

The IEEE 5G/6G Innovation Testbed serves as a valuable resource for academic research and education. By providing access to cutting-edge infrastructure and tools, the Testbed empowers researchers to conduct groundbreaking studies and validate their theoretical findings in a practical setting. This hands-on experience not only deepens their understanding of the underlying technologies but also fosters the development of essential skills required for the workforce of the future.

Furthermore, the Testbed offers unique opportunities for collaborative research projects, enabling cross-disciplinary teams to tackle complex challenges and explore novel solutions. By facilitating the exchange of knowledge and expertise, the Testbed nurtures a culture of innovation and prepares the next generation of researchers and professionals to drive the development of 6G and beyond.

### Academic Perspectives on 6G Standards

As the world eagerly anticipates the advent of 6G technology, the establishment of robust and comprehensive standards is paramount to ensure seamless interoperability, efficient resource utilization, and the realization of the full potential of this groundbreaking wireless communication system. Academic researchers play a pivotal role in shaping these standards, bringing their wealth of knowledge, theoretical expertise, and forward-thinking perspectives to the forefront of [6G development](https://www.comsoc.org/publications/magazines/ieee-wireless-communications/cfp/6g-wireless-technologies-and-systems).

Academic researchers are influencing the establishment of standards for 6G technology through various channels. Many prominent universities and research institutions actively participate in standardization bodies and working groups, contributing their insights and recommendations based on rigorous scientific studies and simulations. These academic contributions span a wide range of areas, including network architecture, air interface design, spectrum allocation, security protocols, and performance metrics.

Moreover, academic researchers often publish their findings and proposed solutions in esteemed journals and conference proceedings, fostering open discourse and facilitating the dissemination of knowledge within the broader scientific community. These publications serve as valuable resources for standardization bodies, providing a solid foundation upon which to build consensus and establish industry-wide guidelines.

When proposing standards for 6G networks, academics prioritize several key considerations. Foremost among these is the pursuit of technological excellence, ensuring that the standards encompass cutting-edge innovations and enable the realization of ambitious performance targets. Academics strive to incorporate theoretical breakthroughs and novel approaches that can unlock unprecedented capabilities, such as ultra-high data rates, ultra-low latency, and massive connectivity.

Additionally, academic researchers emphasize the importance of future-proofing the standards, ensuring that they can accommodate emerging technologies and unforeseen use cases. This forward-thinking approach aims to create a flexible and extensible framework that can adapt to the rapidly evolving technological landscape, minimizing the need for frequent and disruptive overhauls.

Furthermore, academics prioritize the seamless integration of 6G networks with existing [5G advanced](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/10158439) infrastructure, recognizing the need for a smooth transition and backward compatibility. By considering the interoperability and coexistence of these technologies, academic contributions seek to facilitate a gradual and cost-effective migration path for service providers and end-users alike.

Collaboration with standardization bodies plays a vital role in shaping academic contributions in the area of 6G standards. Standards organizations, such as IEEE, provide a platform for academics to engage with industry experts, regulatory bodies, and other stakeholders, fostering a collaborative environment that promotes the exchange of ideas and the reconciliation of diverse perspectives.

Through these collaborative efforts, academic researchers gain valuable insights into real-world constraints, market demands, and regulatory considerations, enabling them to refine their proposals and align their contributions with practical realities. Conversely, standardization bodies benefit from the theoretical rigor and innovative thinking that academics bring to the forefront, ensuring that the established standards are grounded in sound scientific principles and cutting-edge research.

In this evolving technological landscape, academic researchers face several regulatory challenges that must be addressed. One of the most pressing concerns is the allocation and harmonization of spectrum resources for 6G networks. Academics play a crucial role in conducting comprehensive spectrum studies, analyzing the feasibility of utilizing higher frequency bands, and proposing efficient spectrum management strategies.

Furthermore, the integration of emerging technologies, such as artificial intelligence, blockchain, and quantum computing, into 6G systems raises new regulatory challenges related to data privacy, security, and ethical considerations. Academic researchers must navigate these complex issues, collaborating with policymakers and regulatory bodies to develop frameworks and guidelines that strike a balance between technological innovation and societal well-being.

Despite these challenges, the academic community’s commitment to rigorous research and scientific inquiry can help ensure that their contributions to 6G standards are grounded in sound principles and driven by a relentless pursuit of technological excellence. As the world embarks on this transformative journey towards 6G, the perspectives and insights offered by academic researchers will undoubtedly play a pivotal role in shaping the future of wireless connectivity.

### Future Outlook: Academic Contributions to 6G

As the world stands on the verge of the 6G era, academic researchers are tirelessly working towards groundbreaking innovations that will redefine the boundaries of [wireless communication](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9145564). Their unwavering pursuit of knowledge and technological excellence is fueling the development of cutting-edge solutions that promise to revolutionize the way people live, work, and interact with the digital world.

One of the most anticipated breakthroughs in 6G technology that academic researchers are currently working towards is the smooth integration of terrestrial and non-terrestrial networks. By harnessing the power of satellite communications and airborne platforms, 6G aims to provide truly ubiquitous connectivity, transcending geographical barriers and enabling unprecedented coverage and reliability. Academic contributions in areas such as beamforming, interference management, and efficient handover mechanisms are paving the way for this ambitious vision.

Moreover, academic researchers are at the forefront of exploring the potential of Terahertz (THz) and visible light communication (VLC) technologies, which promise to unlock vast breadths of untapped spectrum and enable data rates that surpass the capabilities of [5G technology](https://www.comsoc.org/education-training/media-center/leading-5g-advanced-technology-innovations-6g-era). These groundbreaking advancements not only address the ever-increasing demand for bandwidth but also open the door to a myriad of new applications and services, ranging from holographic communications to wireless brain-computer interfaces.

To stay at the forefront of technological advancements in the transition from 5G to 6G, academia must embrace a multidisciplinary approach and foster collaborations that transcend traditional boundaries. By integrating expertise from diverse fields such as computer science, electrical engineering, materials science, and even neuroscience, academic institutions can catalyze the development of innovative solutions that address the complex challenges of 6G networks.

Furthermore, academia must cultivate an environment that nurtures intellectual curiosity, encourages risk-taking, and celebrates failure as a stepping stone towards success. By embracing a culture of continuous learning and adaptation, academic researchers can remain agile and responsive to the rapidly evolving technological landscape, ensuring that their contributions remain relevant and impactful.

Academic insights will play a pivotal role in shaping the practical implementation and societal impact of 6G networks. As the architects of the underlying theoretical foundations, academic researchers will inform the design and deployment strategies, ensuring that 6G infrastructure is optimized for efficiency, scalability, and sustainability.

Moreover, academic contributions will be instrumental in addressing the ethical and societal implications of 6G technologies, fostering discussions and developing frameworks that ensure responsible innovation and mitigate potential risks. By considering the broader implications of their work, academic researchers can help steer the development of 6G towards outcomes that benefit humanity while safeguarding individual privacy and promoting digital equity.

### Conclusion

The relentless pursuit of innovation in wireless communication has propelled humanity into an era of unprecedented connectivity, where the boundaries of what was once thought possible are continuously redefined. In this ever-evolving landscape, academic institutions and researchers stand as beacons of knowledge and progress, driving the development of 5G technology and laying the groundwork for the transformative potential of 6G networks. Through their unwavering commitment to scientific rigor, interdisciplinary collaboration, and a vision for a more connected future, academia’s contributions will undoubtedly shape the course of technological advancement and leave an indelible mark on the way we experience the digital world.

23-May-2025

# New 5G satellite technology by an international research team advances global mobile connectivity

## Satellite phones and devices are currently the main modes of communication with the rest of the world for remote regions like Antarctica. But that is about to change. Through a unique collaborative research and development effort between industry and academia, regular mobile phones may soon be able to achieve connectivity in such areas.

![Topology of the live video call over 5G NTN technology across Singapore and Japan via GEO satellite. (Source: SUTD)](https://cdn.rohde-schwarz.com/pws/_tech/dam_fallback/fallbackImage_0_320_180_v0.png "Topology of the live video call over 5G NTN technology across Singapore and Japan via GEO satellite. (Source: SUTD)")

Five organizations, the Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD), SKY Perfect JSAT (JSAT), TMY Technology, Inc. (TMYTEK), Rohde & Schwarz, and VIAVI Solutions (VIAVI), have jointly developed a new 5G non-terrestrial network (NTN) satellite technology to advance mobile connectivity in remote locations. The research team conducted a live demonstration today at the World Expo 2025 Singapore Pavilion in Osaka, Japan, showcasing the deployment of an end-to-end cross-country 5G new radio (NR) NTN. This is the first such transmission between the two countries. The live demonstration was witnessed by guest of honor, Mr Ong Eng Chuan, Ambassador of the Republic of Singapore to Japan.

Imagine the scenario, where a ship is sailing in a remote location when a crew member encounters an emergency health issue. With traditional satellite communication, the crew can only send a text message to the shore for assistance. However, with the new technology showcased at the live demonstration, real-time communication via video-call becomes possible. The live demonstration showed that a 5G signal can be transmitted from an end user equipment (UE), such as a communication device, located in SUTD, Singapore, via a satellite antenna, to a geostationary (GEO) satellite operated by JSAT. This signal was then forwarded from the satellite to a ground station in JSAT, Japan, which connects to a 5G base station and 5G core network emulator, demonstrating the feasibility of communications between NTN and terrestrial networks (TN).

The live demonstration successfully showed that an existing GEO satellite can reliably support the 5G NR standards as defined by the Third Generation Partnership Project (3GPP), which is a consortium that develops global standards for mobile telecommunications. Although current 5G deployments primarily rely on TN, upcoming 6G networks are expected to be a convergence of both TN and NTN to achieve global coverage and resilient connectivity. Today’s demonstration will lay the foundation for future extensions to medium earth orbit (MEO) and low earth orbit (LEO) satellites, as well as 6G converged TN and NTN.

Said Professor Tony Quek, Director of Singapore’s Future Communications R&D Program (FCP) and the Head of SUTD’s Information Systems Technology and Design pillar: “As host of the Singapore’s National Future Communications R&D Program (FCP) and its Future Communications and Connectivity Lab (FCCLab), SUTD is proud to bring together partners JSAT, TMYTEK, Rohde & Schwarz, and VIAVI to collaborate and push the boundaries of 5G technology. Each partner brought a different expertise to the research effort and together, we were able to achieve this breakthrough.

“This partnership also provided SUTD students and researchers with valuable hands-on experience working on cutting edge technologies to solve a real-world problem with experts from around the world. I believe this will enable them to enhance their technical abilities as well as develop essential soft skills for the benefit of their future projects,” he added.

“We are proud to have participated in the world's first demonstration using our geostationary satellite and 5G NTN Lab at the prestigious World Expo, under the leadership of SUTD. Moving forward, we will continue to contribute to the advancement of telecommunications technology through collaboration and engagement with stakeholders in Singapore and beyond. Additionally, we will provide a highly reliable communication environment through the Universal NTN, an innovative multi-layered communication platform, aiming to establish technologies that enable seamless connectivity between mobile and satellite networks,” said Mr Eiichi Yonekura, Representative Director, President & CEO of SKY Perfect JSAT.

Additionally, this joint collaboration is also among the first in the world to integrate an electronically steered antenna (ESA) for 5G NTN GEO communications. This enables NTN technology to be more suitable for challenging use cases, such as in the maritime and autonomous vehicles industries; or connecting 5G UE to high-speed moving satellites such as LEO or MEO. Mr Su-Wei Chang, Founder and President of TMYTEK said: “At TMYTEK, we are proud to contribute our ESA technology to this pioneering collaboration, demonstrating our expertise in satellite communications and reinforcing TMYTEK as the go-to partner for 5G NTN connectivity.”

Satellite operators, mobile network operators, equipment vendors and end-user application providers need to be able to evaluate the performance of NTN networks and the traffic that runs across them. Rohde & Schwarz and VIAVI have developed an NTN digital twin testbed covering LEO, MEO and GEO, and this was used in the testing and validation of the end-to-end connectivity and performance in the live demonstration.

Said Dr Sameh Yamany, Chief Technology Officer of VIAVI Solutions: “At VIAVI, we are committed to enabling the future of global connectivity through innovation and collaboration. This breakthrough in 5G NTN technology marks a pivotal moment—not only proving that seamless mobile communication over satellite is achievable, but also laying the groundwork for resilient, borderless 6G networks. We are proud to contribute our test and validation expertise alongside global partners to turn this vision into reality.”

Mr Samuel Lur, General Manager of Rohde & Schwarz said: “As we unveil the latest in 5G NTN technology at the Osaka Expo, Rohde & Schwarz is proud to highlight our contributions in test and measurement that ensure the integrity and performance of these innovations. This achievement is a result of the collective efforts of our esteemed partners at SUTD and other collaborators. Thank you for your dedication and teamwork in pushing the boundaries of technology.”

## Press & media contact

#### Rohde & Schwarz

Rohde & Schwarz is striving for a safer and connected world with its Test & Measurement, Technology Systems and Networks & Cybersecurity Divisions. For over 90 years, the global technology group has pushed technical boundaries with developments in cutting-edge technologies. The company's leading-edge products and solutions empower industrial, regulatory and government customers to attain technological and digital sovereignty. The privately owned, Munich based company can act independently, long-term and sustainably. Rohde & Schwarz generated net revenue of EUR 2.93 billion in the 2023/2024 fiscal year (July to June). On June 30, 2024, Rohde & Schwarz had more than 14,400 employees worldwide.

R&S® is a registered trademark of Rohde & Schwarz GmbH & Co. KG.

About Singapore University of Technology and Design

The Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD) is the world’s first Design AI university. With Design AI, artificial intelligence is treated as a partner and a member of the team – not just a tool. As a result of this unique SUTD treatment, AI and humans brainstorm, spar and prototype together, resulting in solutions that are elevated several-fold. This human-AI team concept has been made possible because of SUTD’s unique cohort-based interdisciplinary pedagogy – which has been in place since the University’s formation in 2009.

As a trailblazer in the field of design and technology education and research, SUTD has been pioneering innovative programmes and initiatives since our inception – including launching the world’s first Design and AI degree in 2020 – well before AI was even a buzzword. The success of that pioneering degree has set the stage for a new growth strategy called SUTD Leap, which was launched in March 2024. Here, SUTD aims to redesign higher education with an even greater focus on design and AI, whilst nurturing the next generation of human-centric design x tech innovators and innovator leaders.

SUTD Website: www.sutd.edu.sg

About SKY Perfect JSAT

Asia's largest geostationary satellite operator, SKY Perfect JSAT, has been delivering reliable communications and broadcasting solutions for over 35 years. Our Space Business provides satellite coverage from North America to the Indian Ocean, supporting both commercial and government connectivity needs. We are expanding our Space Intelligence Business by leveraging satellite data and developing the non-terrestrial network "Universal NTN" to enhance connectivity, contribute to disaster preparedness and national security, and advance toward a super-smart society. In the Media Business, we operate the "SKY PerfecTV!" multi-channel Pay-TV platform in Japan and are extending our services through the Fiber-optic Alliance Business and tailored Media Solutions. We're also exploring Connected TV and Web3 to drive growth in the evolving media landscape.

SKY Perfect JSAT Group Website: www.skyperfectjsat.space/en

Space Business Website: www.skyperfectjsat.space/jsat/en/

About TMY Technology, Inc.

TMY Technology, Inc. (TMYTEK) is a leading provider of electronically steerable antenna (ESA) technology, specializing in design as well as hardware-software integration for sensing and communications. With extensive trial experience in non-terrestrial networks (NTN) and proven beamforming expertise, TMYTEK empowers global partners to achieve seamless, high-performance connectivity. Our cutting-edge phased array solutions support key applications in SATCOM, 5G/B5G, and defense, driving the future of next-generation wireless communication.

TMY Technology, Inc. Website: www.tmytek.com/

About VIAVI

VIAVI (NASDAQ: VIAV) is a global provider of network test, monitoring and assurance solutions for telecommunications, cloud, enterprises, first responders, military, aerospace and railway. VIAVI is also a leader in light management technologies for 3D sensing, anti-counterfeiting, consumer electronics, industrial, automotive, government and aerospace applications. Learn more about VIAVI at www.viavisolutions.com.

## VT News Results

# New industry-university collaboration to pioneer future wireless networks

Virginia Tech and the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative hold leadership positions in the new center.

21 May 2025

group photo

![group photo of attendees at the first meeting of the new WISPER center](/content/news_vt_edu/en/articles/2025/05/industry-university-center-wireless-networks/_jcr_content/article-image.transform/m-medium/image.png)

A new National Science Foundation Industry-University Research Center led by Virginia Tech has funded 10 projects to advance the next generation of wireless networks.

The center is called [WISPER](https://wisperiucrc.org/home/), which stands for Wireless Innovation toward Secure, Pervasive, Efficient, and Resilient NextG networks. [Industry](https://wisperiucrc.org/membership/) members include A2 Labs, BAE Systems, Ericsson, General Dynamics Mission Systems, Hughes Network Systems, InterDigital, Keysight Technologies, Lockheed Martin, Northrop Grumman, Ofinno, RTX, and Trust Web.

The [Commonwealth Cyber Initiative](https://cyberinitiative.org/) (CCI) is facilitating WISPER, which includes founding universities Virginia Tech, George Mason University, and the University of Arizona.

“Wireless networks are a part of our daily lives, underpinning essential functions and keeping us connected,” said Luiz DaSilva, WISPER director and CCI executive director. “Industry and academic partnerships are essential to help create more secure and reliable future networks. Key to this partnership is that industry, through the WISPER Industry Advisory Board, provides guidance and recommendations on the research projects that are funded. These projects provide a great foundation for the new center.”

Robert Gazda, senior director of wireless research at InterDigital, is WISPER’s inaugural Industry Advisory Board chair.

“The collaborative research model championed by WISPER is essential for tackling the most complex challenges in wireless innovation,” Gazda said. “By bridging academic research with real-world industry insights and needs, we can accelerate the development of secure, efficient, and highly scalable technologies that will shape the 6G era and beyond. I’m proud to support this strategic cross-sector partnership, which ensures the wireless solutions we create are not only groundbreaking but truly impactful.”

Top international companies are participating in the center to be at the forefront of advancing the next generation of wireless networks.

“There is increased attention on wireless networks that are reliable, resilient, secure, and trustworthy,” said Mikael Prytz, director of research area networks at Ericsson. “We think there are great opportunities now for society and for our customers as well.”

WISPER is focused on four research themes in next generation networks or NextG: OpenG, SmartG, SpectrumG, and SecureG. “By emphasizing these research areas, WISPER is able to target where industry and customers will benefit the most from university-led research,” said Jacek Kibilda, Virginia Tech site co-director and a faculty member with CCI and the Bradley Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering.

## About the [10 research projects](https://wisperiucrc.org/research-projects/)

**OpenG**

Who Did What? Learning and Reconstructing O-RAN Conflicts with Graph Neural Networks

**SmartG**

Hardware-accelerated Machine Learning Designs for Energy-efficient Real-time Signal Intelligence at the Edge

Multi-Agent Framework for Advanced Topic Intelligence and Search Optimization

**SecureG**

Probabilistic Signal Processing for Integrated Sensing, Communications, and Detection (SPICED)

Secure Beamforming and Tracking in 5G/NextG Systems: Attacks and Countermeasures

Adaptive Modular LLMs for Trustworthy and Efficient On-Device Intelligence in 6G Networks

Wireless Geofencing with Passive Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces

**SpectrumG**

Machine Learning Driven Beam Management for 5G/NextG

QoS-Aware Scheduling for Coexistence of 5G NR-U and WiFi

Synthesis of RF Coverage Maps Using Generative AI

enriched_points.expanded_content: Collaborations between academic institutions and industry ensure the translation of research into practical applications, driving innovation in antenna technology for 5G and 6G networks.

Increased funding from governmental bodies and private sectors accelerates the development of groundbreaking projects, fostering advancements in energy-efficient antennas and next-generation communication technologies.
Research emphasizes sustainable antenna design and energy efficiency, aiming to reduce the environmental impact of 5G/6G technologies while enhancing performance and user experience.
International partnerships facilitate the establishment of global standards for 5G/6G technologies, ensuring interoperability and seamless communication across diverse networks and devices.
